summarization.a: This figure shows the electrical resistivity of TiN ALD films as a function of deposition temperature. For all pulse lengths, resistivity decreases sharply with increasing temperature. Short pulse sequences consistently yield the lowest resistivity at high temperature. Medium pulses show intermediate behavior, while long pulses retain substantially higher resistivity, especially at lower temperatures. The results indicate that both deposition temperature and pulse timing strongly influence TiN film electrical quality.

data_extraction.a:
| Pulse sequence | Deposition temperature (°C) | Resistivity (µΩ·cm) |
|---|---|---|
| Short  | 60  | ~2.5×10⁷ |
| Short  | 120 | ~5×10⁶ |
| Short  | 180 | ~5×10⁴ |
| Short  | 240 | ~1.5×10⁴ |
| Medium | 120 | ~2×10⁷ |
| Medium | 180 | ~3×10⁵ |
| Medium | 240 | ~2×10⁴ |
| Long   | 180 | ~1×10⁷ |
| Long   | 240 | ~3×10⁵ |

a.question: Which pulse sequence yields the lowest resistivity at high deposition temperature (~240 °C)?
How does TiN resistivity change with increasing deposition temperature for all pulse sequences?
a.answer: It decreases by several orders of magnitude.

a.question: Why do shorter pulse sequences result in lower resistivity at elevated deposition temperatures?
a.answer: Shorter pulses limit excessive precursor exposure and reduce residual ligand incorporation in the growing film. At higher temperatures, this favors cleaner surface reactions, improved film densification, and better grain connectivity. These structural improvements reduce electron scattering and lead to lower resistivity compared with longer pulse sequences.

a.question: Would increasing deposition temperature alone fully compensate for using long pulse sequences?
